did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses AI methods, specifically Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and a pre-trained VGG-16 deep neural network, to perform skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset. The results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, computational time) were obtained by training and evaluating these machine learning models.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and comparison of Multi-Layer Perceptron, a custom Convolutional Neural Network, and VGG-16 models on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology sections (Architecture, Datasets), experimental results with quantitative metrics (accuracy, training/testing times), and new findings regarding model performance, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Performance of Multi Layer Perceptron and Deep Neural Networks in Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2021 IEEE 3rd Global Conference on Life Sciences and Technologies (LifeTech 2021)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the performance of Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and a pre-trained VGG-16 model for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset. The researchers trained these models to perform binary classification tasks between specific malignant and benign lesion pairs, such as Melanoma vs. Melanocytic Nevi. Performance was assessed based on classification accuracy (training, validation, and testing) and computational time for both training and testing phases. The results indicate that the pre-trained VGG-16 model achieved the highest classification accuracy among the compared networks. Additionally, while CNN models required longer training times, they demonstrated significantly faster testing times compared to the Multi-Layer Perceptron. The study concludes that VGG-16 offers the best balance of high accuracy and efficient real-time prediction capability for skin cancer classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant lesion included in the HAM10000 dataset; used in binary classification pairs with Melanocytic Nevi and Actinic Keratosis.
Benign lesion included in the HAM10000 dataset; used in binary classification pairs with Melanoma.
Malignant lesion included in the HAM10000 dataset; used in binary classification pairs with Benign Keratosis and Melanoma.
Malignant lesion included in the HAM10000 dataset; used in binary classification pairs with Vascular lesions and Benign Keratosis.
Benign lesion included in the HAM10000 dataset; used in binary classification pairs with Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma.
Pigmented by hemoglobin; included in the HAM10000 dataset; used in binary classification pairs with Basal Cell Carcinoma.
Listed as one of the seven diagnosis categories in the HAM10000 dataset, but explicitly excluded from the binary classification experiments in this study.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of specific skin lesion pairs (Melanoma vs. Melanocytic Nevi, Actinic Keratosis vs. Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Vascular lesions, Melanoma vs. Actinic Keratosis, and Benign Keratosis vs. Basal Cell Carcinoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states it performs 'binary classification of malignant and benign lesions' for five specific classification purposes using the HAM10000 dataset. The models (MLP, custom CNN, VGG-16) predict discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanoma or Melanocytic Nevi) rather than continuous values. Performance is measured by classification accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The 'Human Against Machine with 10000 training images' dataset, a large public dermatoscopic image collection containing 10,015 images collected over 20 years from the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna, Austria, and The Skin Cancer Practice of Cliff Rosendahl in Queensland, Australia.

use.training: Used as the source data for training the Multi-Layer Perceptron, custom CNN, and pre-trained VGG-16 models for binary classification of specific malignant and benign lesion pairs.
use.validation: Used for validation accuracy assessment during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing accuracy and computational time evaluation of the trained models.
use.other: Dermatofibroma (Df) was included in the dataset but explicitly excluded from the binary classification experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (classification accuracy and computational time) without statistical inference.

experiments.classification_pair: Basal Cell Carcinoma (Bcc) and Vascular lesions (Vasc)
Benign Keratosis (Bkl) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (Bcc)

HAM10000.notes: The VGG-16 model achieved the best classification accuracy among the compared networks. CNN models (Custom CNN and VGG-16) required significantly longer training times than MLP but demonstrated much faster testing times. Dermatofibroma (Df) was excluded from the binary classification experiments.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and Pre-trained VGG-16
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG-16 model pre-trained on ImageNet dataset; input shape changed from 224x224 to 128x128 due to long training time.
architecture_used.input_size: 128x128 pixels (for CNN and VGG-16); 1D array (for MLP)

training_details.hardware: Intel Core i3-6006U CPU, 8GB RAM, HP 240 G5 Notebook PC

pairs: Basal Cell Carcinoma (Bcc) vs. Vascular lesions (Vasc)
Benign Keratosis (Bkl) vs. Basal Cell Carcinoma (Bcc)

preprocessing_techniques: Input image resizing to 128x128 pixels (changed from original 224x224 for VGG-16 to reduce training time)
Conversion of input images to 1D arrays for Multi-Layer Perceptron input
conclusions: The pre-trained VGG-16 model achieved the highest classification accuracy among the compared networks (Multi-Layer Perceptron, custom CNN, and VGG-16) across the tested binary classification pairs.
Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models, including the custom CNN and pre-trained VGG-16, demonstrated significantly faster testing (inference) times compared to the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP).
CNN models required substantially longer training times than the MLP due to higher computational complexity and the number of layers used.

Despite longer training times, CNN models are considered more suitable for practical real-time clinical applications because their training can be performed offline, leaving the faster testing time as the critical parameter for real-time prediction.

CNN models outperformed the Multi-Layer Perceptron by a noticeable margin in terms of classification accuracy, with the pre-trained VGG-16 model providing the best balance of high accuracy and efficient real-time prediction capability.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification tasks between specific lesion pairs (e.g., Melanoma vs. Melanocytic Nevi) rather than multi-class classification of all seven HAM10000 classes.
Dermatofibroma (Df) was explicitly excluded from the binary classification experiments, limiting the generalizability of the findings to this specific benign lesion.
The dataset population is biased towards European (Austrian) and Australian demographics, which may affect model performance on other ethnic groups with different skin tones.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons rely solely on descriptive accuracy metrics.
The input image resolution for VGG-16 was reduced from the standard 224x224 to 128x128 to mitigate long training times, which may impact feature extraction quality.
Hardware constraints (Intel Core i3 CPU, 8GB RAM) were used for training, which may not reflect performance on more powerful clinical infrastructure.

The paper argues that CNNs are superior for real-time deployment due to faster testing times despite longer training times, but does not address the computational cost of offline training in resource-limited settings.
Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; the paper does not detail how the HAM10000 dataset was split to ensure no patient overlap between training and testing sets.
